nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, well-lit coral-reef flats and lagoons; typically attached to or embedded in hard coral substrate where intense sunlight reaches the symbiotic zooxanthellae in the mantle.

decorPreferences: stable rockwork with flat perching surfaces for byssal attachment
open sand bed for Derasa or Squamosa placement
high-PAR placement zones (upper rockwork for Maxima/Crocea, sand bed acceptable for Derasa)
reef-grade LED or metal-halide lighting array
moderate, indirect water flow (no direct powerhead blast)

diet.notes: Photoautotrophic via symbiotic zooxanthellae in the mantle — most nutritional needs are met by intense reef lighting. Specimens under approximately 5 cm have been reported to benefit from phytoplankton supplementation several times per week in nutrient-poor (ULNS) systems, as small clams have not yet developed sufficient mantle area to be light-sustained. Larger specimens are generally considered self-sufficient in mature reef tanks with stable nitrate (commonly 2-10 ppm) and phosphate.

avoidWith: angelfishes that nip clam mantles (Pomacanthus, Holacanthus, some Centropyge)
Stomatella-eating fish that may also rasp clam mantle

summary: An umbrella entry for the Tridacna giant-clam trade group (T. maxima, crocea, derasa, squamosa). Photosynthetic reef-tank centerpieces requiring intense lighting, stable reef chemistry, and copper-free systems; CITES Appendix II.
careNotes:
This entry covers the four Tridacna species most commonly traded in the marine aquarium hobby — collectively sold as 'Tridacna clams' or 'giant clams' — under the canonical name Tridacna maxima. Care fundamentals are shared across the group but lighting demand and adult size differ meaningfully:

- **Tridacna maxima (Maxima Clam)** — the trade flagship. High light required (generally advised PAR 200-350+, full reef-grade LED or metal-halide). Adults typically reach roughly 20-30 cm in well-kept tanks; wild specimens have been reported to ~35 cm. Color morphs sold separately include Tahitian, Ultra, Teardrop, Gold, and Squamigera; all share the same care.
- **Tridacna crocea (Crocea Clam)** — the smallest and highest-light Tridacnid. Adults typically reach roughly 12-15 cm. Demands the most intense lighting in the genus and bores into hard substrate using its foot, so should be placed on rock rather than sand.
- **Tridacna derasa (Derasa Clam)** — the largest commonly traded species and generally considered the most light-tolerant. Adults can reach roughly 50-60 cm; juveniles tolerate moderate-to-high lighting and can be placed on the sand bed rather than rockwork. Often recommended as the entry-level Tridacnid.
- **Tridacna squamosa (Squamosa Clam)** — intermediate in both size (to ~40 cm) and lighting demand; identifiable by prominent scalloped scutes on the shell. Tolerates moderate-to-high light and accepts sand or rock placement.

**Shared husbandry requirements:**
- Stable reef chemistry: calcium generally 380-450 ppm (target 400+), magnesium 1280-1350 ppm (target 1300+), alkalinity 8-12 dKH. Tridacnids are calcifiers and are intolerant of swings in any of these parameters.
- Salinity in the standard reef hobby range, generally SG 1.023-1.026.
- Intense, full-spectrum reef lighting; specimens under approximately 5 cm are particularly sensitive and should be photo-acclimated gradually (start on the sand bed and move up over weeks) — direct exposure to a full reef array on day one has been reported to bleach small clams.
- Moderate, indirect water flow. Strong direct currents stress the mantle and cause the clam to close persistently.
- Phytoplankton supplementation is generally advised for clams under roughly 5 cm in low-nutrient systems; larger clams in mature reef tanks are typically self-sustaining.
- **Copper-free system required** — like other molluscs, Tridacnids are highly sensitive to copper-based medications.
- Pyramidellid parasitic snails (small white pyramidal snails on the shell) are the most common Tridacnid pest and should be removed manually at lights-out.

**CITES Appendix II:** All Tridacna species are listed on CITES Appendix II. Legal international trade requires CITES permits and most countries restrict import to aquacultured stock from licensed farms (Tahiti, Solomon Islands, Vietnam, ORA). Keepers should buy aquacultured specimens with documented provenance and check local regulations — some jurisdictions add further restrictions on import or possession.

Source cross-check: Aqua Imports lists temperature 74-78°F (23-26°C) and pH 8.2-8.4; LiveAquaria lists 72-78°F (22-26°C) and pH 8.1-8.4; averaged to 23-26°C and 8.1-8.4 for the hobby-acceptable range. Both sources converge on calcium 380-450 ppm, magnesium 1280-1350 ppm, alkalinity 8-12 dKH, and SG 1.023-1.025. Minimum tank size is not universally specified; given the umbrella covers species reaching 60 cm (Derasa), a 200 L floor is suggested for a Maxima/Crocea and meaningfully larger volumes (400 L+) for Derasa or Squamosa long-term.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Tridacna clams is not practical for home hobbyists; all aquacultured trade stock comes from licensed marine farms in the Indo-Pacific (notably Tahiti, the Solomon Islands, Vietnam, and ORA in the United States). Tridacnids are simultaneous hermaphrodites that broadcast-spawn into open water; larval rearing requires controlled flow-through systems and microalgae cultures beyond home-aquarium scope.

additional.name: LiveAquaria - Maxima Clam Blue Aquacultured
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4), supplements (Ca 380-450 mg/L, Mg 1280-1350 ppm, dKH 8-12), lighting (high-intensity required, photo-acclimation for specimens under 2 inches), and reef-safe status. Origin: aquacultured Central/South Pacific.

additional.name: LiveAquaria - Giant Clams for Beginners (article)
additional.notes: Source for genus-wide care guidance and inter-species comparison. T. derasa parameters: 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, Ca 400-450 ppm. Confirmed Derasa as the largest and most light-tolerant Tridacnid (max ~20 inches / 50 cm).

additional.name: Wikipedia - Maxima clam
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Cardiidae, Cardiida), native range (Indo-Pacific including East Africa, Red Sea, and Pacific islands), adult size (~20 cm typical large specimens), and CITES Appendix II listing requiring permits for international trade.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: High lighting and chemistry-stability requirements plus CITES Appendix II legal complexity — only appropriate for reef keepers with intense reef lighting, stable calcium/magnesium/alkalinity dosing, and documented aquacultured provenance.
